speaker: interviewer
text: A few moments.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't think that I'm going to last that long. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about the German people now? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: To answer this questions-- this question, I have never been to Germany again. When we went the first time to Israel, I really looked for cheaper fare because we were four people at that time and we stayed, and we planned to stay in a hotel. When I talked to a travel agent, I was told if I go to-- to Frankfurt and buy a ticket in Frankfurt to Israel, I can save like $100.00 or so a person, or maybe more. And I said, my feet are not going to step on the German soil again. And we did not go. We took-- we took a plane, came to-- I think to-- we landed in France, and from France we went to-- to Israel. And going back, we went with El Al straight back to-- to Los Angeles. The same thing with Poland. I don't think, and I know a lot of people go back to see, how it's-- to show their children where they live, and if there is a grave. I know that I don't have my parents' grave there. I have a grandmother that died. My father's mother died during the ghetto, because she lost her youngest son. She was a lady of 86 years old, and her grave was not even marked because was already in the ghetto. So I don't think it would be possible to find even a grave of my uncle, of my grandmother. And to go back there, I could not-- I could not give them one of my hard earned money to spend in Poland. Because if I am bitter, I'm bitter because it could be different. If these people would have been educated a different way, if they were not given to hatred of this is a Jew, you have to hate that Jew, I think that the times would have been much different. The Germans would not have an easy way to dispose of so many Jews, to kill so many Jews, because they were very much helped by the Polish people, very much. Later, they found out that after the Jews, they started persecuting Poles too, not only Jews. That took them a long time to find this out. In the meantime, it was a huge population of Jewish people lost in the meantime. And even after the war, I heard-- I was never there-- but I heard that if there was-- meat was-- beef was rationed, and if they came to the line and was not-- there was not enough to go about, they said that the Jews are not working and they stay in the line and they get all the beef. So if the Polish come to the line, they cannot get any beef. So this is still anti-Semitism in their heart. You-- I think it will take maybe, maybe another generation. If they can be taught, if they can go and see what can happen to a nation, what can happen to people, what can happen to musicians, to artists, to doctors, to lawyers, to mothers, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe this will teach them a lesson. I hope that what you people are doing now will bring good things to people, will maybe swell their hearts, cure their hearts, so maybe we will never have another Holocaust again. And I pray to God before Rosh Hashanah that God should really see to it that no human soul should be persecuted or killed by another human being. And that goes for wars, too. I hope that people will get smart and be able to live in peace with one another, and love one another. And I want to thank you for letting me do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: We-- we need to thank you for sharing your story, but we're not done yet. I-- I wanted to ask you-- you-- we-- we talked a little bit about your father earlier, and I know that he didn't go with you to-- to Auschwitz, he was left in the Lodz Ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was-- as I said before, we found him very sick when-- when he was hiding in a different place, and we were hiding in a different place. And we went to look for him. We found him in a attic, laying on a-- on a table. And he told us that the Germans came in to look for people, and they found him there, but he was so weak, and so sick, that they-- he said they asked him to get off the table, and he said that he cannot make it. And he looked, his face was like of an angel. The look-- his looks of it was like an angel face with a beard. And they looked at him and they let him stay there, and they walked out. They didn't kill him. He said to them, he says, I can't get up, you want you can kill me, but they walked out. They didn't kill him. And when we came back to look for him, we kept start asking people if they saw him some place. We knew approximately where he was. So when we came there, and we found somebody and he says, oh, he must be in the attic there. So we went up to the attic and we found him unconscious.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my sister and I ran down and we found a wheelbarrow, a three wheel. And we took that wheelbarrow, and we carried him down the stairs. And we put him in the wheelbarrow, and we went to the-- to the room where we had before to get the blanket because he was so frail, that I was afraid that his bones might break when we wheel him from the metal of that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- the ghetto was pitch black, there was no light, and there were a lot of wires because people used to make little gardens where they were planting some vegetables. But this was all torn apart so the wires were just laying around. And you-- you didn't-- you couldn't see them. You could kill yourself. But somehow, we got to [? Marachine ?]. It took a half a night. My mother even said that she gave out-- up on us already. She did not know that we found my father, but she thought that we-- we were gone some place. We came there, and my father, we nourished him and he got better, but he was very, very weak. And the day when we were leaving, he was very sick, and he said that he won't be able to go with us because he knows that he's going to be a burden. And not only that, he says he's going to die, and he would rather die where he lived all his life. Although he did not live in the ghetto, but was the city that he lived all his life. And he-- like-- like, he would order us, you go. You go. You save yourself. You go. And we went, and my sister Perl, God rest her soul, when we were already in the train, she ran back to the ghetto to persuade him again to come. And he said, no, you go and save yourself. Unfortunately, she died just shortly before the liberation. And my father could not make it even to the train, and not enough. In that train, he would have died in the train. So I think, God blessed him that he could stay. Whichever way he died, we don't know if he was shot, or he died, or somebody found him. We will never find this out, what happened to him. But he deserved-- he was a good man, a good Jew, and he deserved a different death. But, this was something that nothing-- nothing could have helped. It was destination and that's what we have to-- to cope with. And I hope that our children will remember, and I know they will. And I know my grandchildren will be good people. And I hope that someday, they can teach, and talk to people, and tell them of our experience so maybe it will got-- be a better world to live in.

speaker: interviewer
text: They already are. Is there anything we didn't talk about that-- that's important that you would like to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, just-- I think we-- most of things that we talked about, I just want to repeat again, what wonderful parents, what wonderful memories I have of my childhood. And I hope that my children will have good memories about us when we will-- won't be here anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to show some of your pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, I'd be happy to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, on this picture, the bottom line, is my sister Jochewed. She's called now Judy, and she lives in Los Angeles. In the middle is my sister, Devora, and she was killed by a-- by a Polish officer when she was going to look for a place to hide in the forest. The next one is my sister Mindel. And in back of her, in the hat, is her husband [? Karl ?]. They had two children and they all perished in the ghetto. On my left, in the back, is my oldest sister Raica, with her husband, [? Mayu ?]. They escaped to Warsaw. And when I came to Warsaw, they lived in one room, but they took me in like my mother and father. They cared for me like a small child. I was very sick at that time, and they nourished me back to health. None of them-- in the middle, there is a cousin, who immigrated to Israel and became a professor of the university, and he's also not alive anymore but at least he saved himself, to live happy years in Israel. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my sister Perl. She was with us in the ghetto. She worked as a nurse-- as a nurse with the children on [? Marachine, ?] and when they-- they asked her to put the children on the wagons, she got so sick that she had to be taken to the hospital. She came to Auschwitz and we saw only twice. And we found in a-- in a-- somebody sent me a newspaper years ago, that her-- her grave was discovered on a cemetery. And it was in the forward, but I could never get in touch with the person who found her, and she died just shortly before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this--

speaker: interviewer
text: When he gives you this--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is my sister Perl, Perl Landowicz, and this picture was taken just shortly before the war, approximately 1939. She was with us together in the ghetto. This picture was taken-- taken in Lodz, and Perl died in 1944. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture is of my three sisters with a cousin. And this was taken in Lodz when Israel came to say good bye before he left for Israel to attend the University of Jerusalem. This was approximately 1932. To my left is my older sister Raica, and then is Israel, the cousin. Next one is Devora and Mindel. They all perished in 1942. Israel lived in Israel and was teaching at the University of Jerusalem, and he died just a few years ago. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was at a bris, and it was in 1989. To my left, is my oldest sister, my oldest living sister, Jochewed, who's called now Judy. The next to her is my youngest sister, Ruth, and I'm next to her, Sally. Ruth-- Judy is Judy [? Schumener ?], Ruth is Ruth [? Miller, ?] and I'm Sally [? Marco ?]. Next to her in the center is Ruth's daughter, Estelle, with David in her arms, who was just newly born. Next to-- to Estelle, in the print dress, is my sister Regina, who's next to me. And her name is Regina [? Hirsch. ?] And next to her is a friend that-- that joined the family. And the one in the red dress is Regina's daughter [? Lorraine. ?] This was done in 1989. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture was taken in 1992, in Los-- as a matter of fact, in Beverly Hills in the restaurant where we celebrated Ben's 80th birthday. To my right-- to my left is Sally Marco, myself. Next to me is Ben, my husband. In the back, in the middle, is Brian, my son-in-law, our son-in-law. Next to him is Estelle, our daughter, and holding-- holding [? Gerone ?] the second grandchild. Next to [? Gerone ?] is Noah Marco, our son. And next to him is Connie, Connie [? Marco-Calt ?]. And in the center, on the bottom, is David [? Ashgenaze ?], our oldest grandson. This was taken at Ben's 80th birthday in Beverly Hills in 1992. We had a nice time and all the gifts went to [? Demartis ?] Memorial, donate. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture was also taken in Beverly Hills, California. Was the wedding of our daughter, Estelle Marco to Brian [? Ashgenaze ?]. And it was in December of 1986. To my left, is my husband Ben, the very happy, cheerful face, happy to marry a daughter off. The bride, of course, is Estelle [? Marco-Ashgenaze ?]. And her husband is Brian [? Ashgenaze ?]. Next to Brian is Sally Marco, myself. And next to me is our son Noah Marco. And we were very happy, we were a very happy united family, at that time. This picture was taken in Los Angeles, and on another tape that my husband Ben made, we showed Noah's and Connie's wedding picture, but not to leave them out on this tape, we're just showing two happy people on a picture. This was taken 1992 in Los Angeles. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, should I go again?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Noah, our son, and Connie, his wife. And we are very happy to have them in the family. This is '94 so it's '90. OK. This is a picture of our pride and joy. These are our three grandsons. The oldest one is David-- David-- no, David-- David-- [? Gerone-- ?] No. Oh, gosh. OK. OK. This is our pride and joy, our three grandsons. The oldest one is going to be seven at the end of December, his name is David [? Arie ?] [? Ashgenaze ?]. Next to him is [? Gerone ?] [? Eton ?] [? Ashgenaze ?]. He's going to be, on Rash Hashanah, five years old. And in the middle is [? Ilan ?] [? Akiva ?] [? Ashgenaze ?], and he's going to be two years old on Yom Kippur. So we know, we always celebrate their birthdays, one on the Jewish New Year's, one on the [INAUDIBLE] New Year's, and one on Yom Kippur. Ilan is going to be two years old. And this picture was taken in Los Angeles in 1993. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture is of my husband Ben as a Polish soldier. And we-- we did not show it before, this was probably taken around 1938. And he just found it, so we are very proud to have it. He looks at himself, reminding himself of the good old days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Poland, and he was a soldier of the Polish Army at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was his-- his name was Marco then also, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, his name Berek Mordkowicz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mordkowicz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.
